cards.title: A.P.B. — the 3 ad set decisions
cards.front: Every single ad set forces three core decisions. What are they?
cards.back: A.P.B. = Audience, Placement, Budget — the three core decisions every ad set requires, regardless of campaign objective (a play on law enforcement's "all-points bulletin"). You're describing the "suspect"/prospect you want to engage. Some objectives add extra components on top of A.P.B.

cards.title: Why the ad set level is where the work happens
cards.front: Which level of a Meta campaign is where most testing and tweaking actually happens?
cards.back: The ad set level is where most ongoing changes/modifications are made and where the majority of variable testing happens. Because of the flexibility at this level, large accounts can have hundreds or thousands of ad sets per campaign.

cards.title: The Four Ad Set Groups
cards.front: What four audience buckets make a campaign fire "on all four cylinders"?
cards.back:
The Four Ad Set Groups (Targeting Game Plan) — target all four:
- 1. Your Fans (warm) — strong ROI + social proof, only if fans were generated the right way
- 2. Your Custom Audiences (warm) — better ROI than cold since they know your brand; power grows with size
- 3. 1% Lookalike Audiences (cold) — great new-prospect source; more powerful as the source audience grows
- 4. Interests and Behaviors (cold) — where most campaigns start; 5–20 interests recommended

cards.title: Sequenced rollout of the four groups
cards.front: You don't have all four audience types yet. Which order do you add them in?
cards.back:
Sequenced rollout:
- 1. Start with Ad Set #4 — Interest Targeting
- 2. As you build audiences, add #1 (Fans) and #2 (Custom Audiences)
- 3. As you see conversions from #1 and #2, add #3 (1% Lookalikes)
This is the general best starting reference — customize to your business. Don't worry if you don't have all assets yet; this is what to aim for.

cards.title: One interest per ad set
cards.front: Why split interests into separate ad sets instead of stacking them together?
cards.back: Keep one (or two related) interest per ad set so you can measure how each interest performs — split interests into separate ad sets rather than combining them. Target ~1.5M reach per ad set for clean, comparable data. Each ad set should still contain multiple ads for a good spread (except the Michigan Method).

cards.title: How many interests to start with
cards.front: How many interest-targeted ad sets should your budget support?
cards.back:
Rule: daily ad spend ÷ 10 ≈ number of ad sets/interests (e.g., $50/day ÷ 10 ≈ 5 ad sets).
- Large budget: ~20 interests
- Small budget: ~5 interests
- General recommended interest range: 5–20 interests
- Reach target per interest ad set: ~1.5 million

cards.title: Where ads serve & the two placement modes
cards.front: Across which networks can your ads run, and what are your two ways to choose placement?
cards.back:
Ads serve across Facebook, Instagram, the Audience Network, and Sponsored Messages, via two modes:
- Automatic placements — Facebook chooses (recommended default)
- Edit placements — you choose device and platform

cards.title: Use Automatic placements for cold traffic
cards.front: When is letting Facebook fully control placement actually the smart move?
cards.back: Automatic placements = Facebook's recommended default, giving the algorithm full control. A smart strategy for new campaigns to cold traffic — it reveals where your audience responds most, which you can then apply to future manual placements.

cards.title: How Edit placements cascade
cards.front: Why does choosing 'desktop only' make Instagram disappear from your placement options?
cards.back: Edit placements lets you control device and platform, but choices cascade: device type (all / mobile only / desktop only) and campaign objective both constrain available platforms (e.g., desktop-only removes Instagram, which is mobile-only). FB placements: feeds, instant articles, in-stream videos, right column, suggested videos. IG placements: feed or Stories.

cards.title: Advanced placement controls
cards.front: Beyond platform and device, what three extra placement levers can you pull?
cards.back:
Advanced placement controls:
- 1. Specific mobile devices/OS (all mobile, Android only, iOS only, and/or WiFi-only)
- 2. Exclude content categories that conflict with your brand on in-stream video, audience network, and instant articles
- 3. Block lists for specific websites/apps/publishers — once uploaded, reusable across multiple campaigns

cards.title: Daily vs. Lifetime budget
cards.front: Daily or Lifetime budget — which do you pick, and what locks once you choose?
cards.back:
Budget is set at the ad set level:
- Daily — recommended for ongoing control
- Lifetime — recommended for fixed-duration campaigns with a hard cap; also unlocks ad scheduling (dayparting)
You cannot switch types after choosing — you must duplicate the ad set to change.

cards.title: Daily budget can overspend by 25%
cards.front: Facebook spent more than your daily budget today — did it break your cap?
cards.back: Daily budget is treated as an average: Facebook can spend up to 25% over on a single day, but it averages out over a 7-day window, so you never overspend the overall budget.
cards.example: $100/day cap can hit $125 on a high-opportunity day, balanced by lower days.

cards.title: Ad scheduling & launch timing
cards.front: When should you daypart your ads — and why never launch late in the day?
cards.back: Lifetime budget unlocks ad scheduling (dayparting). Running ads all the time usually performs best (the algorithm serves during the best windows), but scheduling lets you control exact times when needed. Use scheduled start/end dates to time launches/stops. Tip: don't launch late in a day — use a scheduled start to go live early the next morning.

cards.title: Automatic vs. Manual bid
cards.front: When would you override Facebook's automatic bid with a manual one?
cards.back:
Bid amount is set in optimization & delivery:
- Automatic bid — let Facebook get the most results (the usual choice)
- Manual bid — sets your target cost per result, with AVERAGE or MAXIMUM options
Use Manual MAXIMUM when you have a hard cap on cost per result.

cards.title: Bid types: CPC, CPM, oCPM
cards.front: Three bid types exist — which is the default for almost every objective?
cards.back:
Bid types:
- CPC — charged only on click; you set the max, starting at 1¢
- CPM — pay per 1,000 impressions whether clicked or not; mainly brand advertising, not best for most advertisers
- oCPM — shows your ad to people most likely to take your desired action, auto-adjusting bid within budget
oCPM is the default for all objectives except Brand Awareness and Reach.

cards.title: Your bid is only one auction factor
cards.front: After a few thousand impressions, what besides your bid starts moving your cost?
cards.back: Your ad competes in an auction against everyone targeting the same audience; bid is only one factor. After a few thousand impressions, CTR, Relevance Score, and likes/complaints affect cost. Facebook often charges less than you bid. A credit card is required before ads display.

cards.title: What each objective needs beyond A.P.B.
cards.front: Some objectives ask for more than Audience/Placement/Budget. Which need what?
cards.back:
Objective-specific settings (beyond A.P.B.):
- Brand awareness, post engagement, event response, video views, store visits → A.P.B. only
- Reach, page likes, lead generation → choose the Page
- App installs → choose your app; Product catalog → choose your products
- Traffic → choose destination (website / Messenger / app); optional offer (pick the Page, then an existing or new offer)
- Conversion → choose the conversion event to optimize for

cards.title: Like campaign budget & settings
cards.front: How much of total spend should a fan-building (Like) campaign eat?
cards.back:
Like campaign rules:
- Start at $10/day per ad set (a little less is fine), keep bidding automatic
- Should consume only 5–10% of total budget (up to 20–25% in exceptional high-conversion cases)
- Name each ad set by its targeting + placement; update the name if you change targeting/placement before going live
- Set it and let it run so your audience always grows with fresh leads
cards.example: Ad set name: U.S., CA, GB, AU, NZ, Women 25 to 45, Android

cards.title: Like campaign placement rule
cards.front: Why should a Like campaign just use Automatic Placements?
cards.back: For Like campaigns, Instagram, Audience Network, and Messenger are ineligible — just use Automatic Placements. Mobile-only / non-mobile / device restrictions are allowed but rarely needed.

cards.title: The one time you run multiple ads per ad set
cards.front: Normally one ad per ad set in tests — when is multiple ads actually correct?
cards.back: For smaller audiences (under ~400K, or under the 1M+ ideal): keep multiple ad variations in rotation inside one ad set but only one ad live at a time. This is the only time more than one ad per ad set is recommended — it lets you swap ads when frequency gets too high and CPA starts increasing. Keep budgets small; you may need to switch bidding from conversions to impressions.

cards.title: Carousel: best-image-first or not?
cards.front: For a step-by-step story carousel, do you check or uncheck "show best performing image first"?
cards.back:
Carousel ordering:
- Uncheck "show best performing image first" for sequential/story carousels meant to be viewed in order (e.g., the DM HQ steps carousel)
- Check it for feature/benefit carousels with no required order (e.g., TruConversion's software-benefit screens)
cards.example: TruConversion hook: "it's easy to double your sales when you know exactly what your customers want."

cards.title: The Michigan Method
cards.front: A method runs up to 180 ad sets with exactly one ad each. What is it and why?
cards.back: The Michigan Method (part of the Five-Tier Scaling System) — many small-budget ad sets, sometimes as many as 180, with only ONE ad per ad set. Target larger audiences (400,000–2 million) so ad sets don't cannibalize one another or saturate the market. Within a short period you see which ad sets fail and which deliver an awesome CPA: "Killing the Ponies, and Feeding the Stallions." The five-tier scaling system is about "bulking-up your stallions."
cards.example: Advanced: duplicate the entire 20-interest campaign and swap in 20 new interests, keeping the same creative.

cards.title: Duplicate at the ORIGINAL budget
cards.front: You scaled a winner from $10 to $20/day. At what budget do you launch its duplicates?
cards.back: Don't duplicate at the increased budget. When duplicating a winning ad set, start the copy at the original starting budget, not the later-raised one — duplicating at the increased budget produces worse results. Example: original raised from $10/day to $20/day → duplicates should be $10/day, not $20/day.

cards.title: Duplicate ad sets to target many interests fast
cards.front: Fastest way to spin up 10 interest ad sets from one built ad set?
cards.back:
Power User Tip — duplicate ad sets:
- Click the paused campaign to open the Ad Sets tab
- Select the first ad set and click Duplicate
- Leave default Keep Same Campaign selected
- Set number of duplicates to one less than your total interests (10 interests → 9 duplicates → 10 total)
- For status, choose Keep Campaign Status Same as Duplicated
- Find the new ad sets ending in the word Copy
- Select each copy, hit Edit, and add a new/different audience/interest

cards.title: Duplicate a campaign for new targeting groups
cards.front: In Power Editor, how do you clone a campaign for a fresh targeting group?
cards.back:
Power Editor — duplicate a campaign:
- Find the campaign and select Duplicate at the top
- Keep the same campaign — keep the status option checked
- Hit Create; the new campaign appears with "Copy" in the title (don't retitle yet)
- Change only the targeting in this ad set (add custom audiences once you have them; for a new brand, add more interests and watch reach change)
- Repeat to create more ad sets for different interests; each keeps multiple ads for spread/data
- Press Review Changes, hit Continue, then confirm all ad sets appear in Ads Manager

cards.title: One-Ad Experiment foundational setup
cards.front: What two starter campaigns let Facebook find your winning creative cheaply?
cards.back:
One-Ad Experiment foundational setup:
- Quickly build the landing pages
- Create the new Business Manager
- Set up tracking/pixel
- Launch Campaign 1: a Like Ad (Page Likes) campaign with 3 different images to build fans cheaply
- Launch Campaign 2: a long-copy Link Post Ad Website Conversions campaign with 2 different images to drive subscribers/conversions
- Let Facebook find the winning creative within each campaign

cards.title: Don't stack interests when comparing
cards.front: What's the cost of cramming many interests into one ad set?
cards.back: Don't combine many interests in one ad set when you want to compare — you lose per-interest performance visibility. Keep one (or two related) interests per ad set.

cards.title: Two setup traps: CPM & budget type lock
cards.front: Two easy setup mistakes — picking the wrong bid type and assuming budget type is editable. What are they?
cards.back:
Avoid these traps:
- Don't pick CPM for most advertisers — it's brand-advertising-oriented (pay per view whether or not anyone clicks) and not best for most goals
- Don't expect to switch budget types in place — daily vs. lifetime can't be changed after selection; you must duplicate the ad set
